Applications to conditional volatility models, including standard and threshold generalised autoregressive conditional heteroskedasticity models, demonstrate substantial computational speed-ups while maintaining inferential accuracy. The proposed approach outperforms uniform subsampling and compares favourably with recent stochastic gradient and divide-and-conquer MCMC methods for dependent data.

title: Sensitivity Quantification for Distribution System State Estimation
authors: Betül Mamudi, Jochen Stiasny, Jochen Cremer
abstract: Pseudo-measurements are the dominant source of uncertainty in distribution system state estimation (DSSE), yet their distributional assumptions are treated as fixed inputs by existing uncertainty quantification methods. This paper investigates whether the uncertainty bounds assumed by weighted least squares (WLS)-based DSSE are sensitive to these distributional assumptions, and whether this sensitivity is quantifiable using the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We propose a diagnostic framework that compares the true Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) against the WLS-assumed CRB via a per-bus, per-scenario ratio, computed directly from the converged WLS solution. Pseudo-measurement distributions are varied across five types in 22 variants matched at equal spread to isolate shape effects from variance. Experiments on the CIGRE MV network across 100 operating scenarios yield three findings. First, heavy-tailed and skewed distributions show consistently that WLS systematically overstates its uncertainty bounds. Second, the degree of miscalibration varies across buses and operating scenarios, confirming that distributional sensitivity is not uniform. Third, the CRB ratio is structurally blind to mean-shift bias, exposing a fundamental limitation of variance-based uncertainty diagnostics. Together, these results confirm the hypothesis and show that the choice of pseudo-measurement distribution directly distorts the confidence limits under WLS-based assumptions, which must be explicitly accounted for in any uncertainty-aware DSSE method.

title: Support-Conditioned Flow Matching Is Kernel Smoothing
authors: Daniel Matsui Smola
abstract: Generative models are often conditioned on a small set of examples via cross-attention. Under the Gaussian optimal-transport path, we show that the exact velocity field induced by a finite support set is a Nadaraya--Watson kernel smoother whose bandwidth decreases with flow time, from broad averaging at early steps to nearest-neighbor at late steps. A single Gaussian-kernel attention head exactly computes this field, connecting cross-attention conditioning to classical kernel theory. The theory predicts three failure regimes: nearest-neighbor collapse of the kernel at high dimension, mismatch between the isotropic kernel and the data geometry, and insufficient support for nonparametric estimation. Experiments on Gaussian mixtures, spherical shells, and DINOv2 ImageNet features confirm that learned conditioning improves in precisely these regimes, and that IP-Adapter's cross-attention implements approximate NW smoothing in practice.

title: Teaching and Learning under Deductive Errors
authors: Jan Arne Telle, Brigt Håvardstun, Jose Hernandez-Orallo
abstract: Most models of machine teaching and learning assume the learner makes no errors in its internal deductive inference. However, humans and large language models in few-shot learning regimes are two important examples of learners where this does not hold. They fail on some consistency checks, and they can fail stochastically. In this paper we introduce a teaching and learning framework that takes these deductive errors into account. We specifically study the case of machine teaching, as different characterizations of the teacher can account for both machine teaching and learning. In an overhauled Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) setting, we study theoretically that, for some estimated error level, the teacher must find a PAC teaching set that with high probability will lead the learner to guess a hypothesis that is approximately correct. We study the computational complexity of six different problems related to computing optimal PAC teaching sets. We give XP algorithms parametrized by size of teaching set, with tight runtime bounds under standard complexity assumptions like ETH. These results are complemented with a small experimental study of which teaching and learning protocols can best represent the observed behavior in some LLM teaching sessions.

title: Beyond Oversquashing: Understanding Signal Propagation in GNNs Via Observables
authors: Eden Nagar, Ya-Wei Eileen Lin, Ron Levie
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) perform computations on graphs by routing the signal between graph regions using a graph shift operator or a message passing scheme. Often, the propagation of the signal leads to a loss of information, where the signal tends to diffuse across the graph instead of being deliberately routed between regions of interest. Two notions that depict this phenomenon are oversmoothing and oversquashing. In this paper, we propose an alternative approach for modeling signal propagation, inspired by quantum mechanics, using the notion of observables. Specifically, we model the place in the graph where the signal lies, how much the signal is concentrated there, and how much of the signal is propagated towards a location of interest when applying a GNN. Using these new concepts, we prove that standard spectral GNNs have poor signal propagation capabilities. We then propose a new type of spectral GNN, termed Schrödinger GNN, which we show has a superior capacity to route the signal across the graph.

title: LoREnc: Low-Rank Encryption for Securing Foundation Models and LoRA Adapters
authors: Beomjin Ahn, Jungmin Kwon, Chanyong Jung, Jaewook Chung
abstract: Foundation models and low-rank adapters enable efficient on-device generative AI but raise risks such as intellectual property leakage and model recovery attacks. Existing defenses are often impractical because they require retraining or access to the original dataset. We propose LoREnc, a training-free framework that secures both FMs and adapters via spectral truncation and compensation. LoREnc suppresses dominant low-rank components of FM weights, compensates for the missing information in authorized adapters, and further applies orthogonal reparameterization to obscure structural fingerprints of the protected adapter. Unauthorized users produce structurally collapsed outputs, while authorized users recover exact performance. Experiments demonstrate that LoREnc provides strong protection against model recovery with under 1% computational overhead.

title: Almost Affine Invariance Over Prime Fields: Green Problem 90
authors: Jie Ma, Quanyu Tang, Max Wenqiang Xu
abstract: Let $A\subset \mathbb{F}_p$ with density 1/2. We call a set $A$ almost affine invariant under an affine transformation $φ(x)=ax+b$ if \[|A \triangle φ(A)| =o(p).\] We determine that, the threshold value of $K$ such that $A$ is almost affine invariant simultaneously under all $φ(x)$ with $|a|, |b|\le K$ and $a\neq 0$, is $K=o(\log p)$. This solves Ben Green's Open Problem 90.

title: Dipole light-matter interactions in the bispinor formalism
authors: Sebastian Golat, Alex J. Vernon, Francisco J. Rodríguez-Fortuño
abstract: The conventional formulation of power absorption, optical forces, and torques on dipolar particles involve lenghty and cumbersome expressions that obscure their shared physical origin. We apply a bispinor formalism that unifies these disparate phenomena in a very general case including chiral and nonreciprocal particles. This reveals that force, torque, absorbed power, and absorbed helicity rate can all be concisely expressed in terms of broken symmetries, and leads to the fundamental inequalities that dipolar particles' cross-sections must satisfy. This framework uncovers profound connections normally hidden behind complex algebra -- for instance, pressure forces depend exclusively on the difference in linear momenta of different light components and the corresponding breaking of symmetry by a particle, and optical recoil forces depend exclusively on helicity cross sections -- providing clarity, conciseness, and a powerful predictive tool for arbitrary dipole interactions.

title: Implementing Fluid Antennas in the Beamspace: Performance Evaluation and Codebook Design
authors: Pablo Ramírez-Espinosa, F. Javier López-Martínez, David Morales-Jiménez
abstract: Metasurface-based fluid antenna systems (FASs) have been recently proposed as an inexpensive, scalable and practical alternative implementation for the fluid-antenna concept. This work thoroughly evaluates the performance of metasurface-based FASs in the context of multi-user communications. We extend the state-of-the-art signal model of FASs to electronically-reconfigurable designs, explicitly including the antenna response in the equivalent channel and resulting correlation structure. A general codebook design procedure, accounting for practical aspects like reflections and radiation efficiency, is presented and used to design the different antenna configurations (regarded as FAS ports). Importantly, we show that, with proper design, metasurface-based FASs can significantly outperform conceptual ones. While state-of-the-art theoretical embodiments of FAS rely on spatial flexibility for constructive/destructive interference, metasurface-based FASs exploit interference cancellation through projection onto the interference null space. Numerical results show a remarkable improvement when the system is dominated by interference (i.e., the natural FASs operational regime), regardless of spatial propagation characteristics.

title: Memristor-Based Immune Neural Network Circuit With Neuromorphic Learning and Self-Repairing and its Application in Industrial Fault Diagnosis
authors: Kefan Tao, Yanfeng Wang, Junwei Sun
abstract: Existing memristive brain-inspired circuits exhibit relatively simplistic associative memory mechanisms, which neglect the integration with other systems of biological organisms. In this article, a memristor-based immune neural network circuit with neuromorphic learning and self-repairing is designed, and semisupervised learning and allergy learning are also taken into account. The designed circuit is mainly composed of semisupervised learning module, allergy learning module, neural regulation module, emotional learning module, and immune neural network module. The processes of semisupervised learning and neural regulation are investigated into the implementation framework of immune learning. Moreover, the allergy learning process classified as excessive immune response is also included, which allows for rapid adaptive adjustments after an allergy response occurs. In addition, both excessive immune activation and immune system damage are taken into consideration, and the regulatory effects of emotional factors on the immune learning process are studied. The feasibility of the circuit is verified by Personal Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis, and the proposed circuit provides a reference for biomimetic robots in industrial fault diagnosis.

title: An SDMT-Based CMOS Voltage Reference With a Novel NCC Scheme for PSRR Bandwidth Enhancement
authors: Qibin Chen, Jin Xie, Gelin Zhou, Jianwei Zhang, Xingxing Pan, Hui Cai, Jinhui Chen, Jinghu Li, Zhicong Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a CMOS voltage reference (VR) based on the stacked diode-connected MOS transistor (SDMT), which incorporates a novel negative capacitance compensation (NCC) technique to enhance the bandwidth of the power supply rejection ratio (PSRR). The PSRR bandwidth of conventional SDMT-based CMOS VRs is restricted by the parasitic capacitance from the gate of the PMOS current source to ground. To address this issue, a cross-coupled pair negative capacitance circuit is applied to the gate of the PMOS current source. This technique reduces the total gate-to-ground capacitance, shifting the dominant PSRR zero to higher frequencies and effectively extending the PSRR bandwidth. It provides a novel approach and reference for enhancing the PSRR bandwidth of voltage References (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V_{\text {REF}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). The proposed VR was implemented in a 65-nm CMOS process, occupying a silicon area of 0.0045mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Measurement results demonstrate that the NCC technique extends the PSRR bandwidth from 650Hz to 5.9kHz, achieving an approximately 9-fold improvement. The average untrimmed temperature coefficient (TC) of 8 samples is 45.2ppm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$/ ^{\circ } $</tex-math> </inline-formula>C in a temperature range from −40°C to 125°C while only consuming 30 nW.

title: EAGLE: A Flexible Heterogeneous Analog Compute-In-Memory Architecture With RISC-V Programmable Multi-Core Accelerators
authors: Elena Ferro, Alessandro Ottaviano, Corey Lammie, Gamze İslamoğlu, Angelo Garofalo, Francesco Conti, Thomas Boesch, Luca Benini, Abu Sebastian, Irem Boybat
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: In today’s AI-driven landscape, designing hardware to accelerate deep learning inference faces challenges in meeting growing demands for speed, computational efficiency, and flexibility. Emerging technologies like Analog Compute-In-Memory (ACIM) hold significant promise for enhancing computational efficiency in these workloads, particularly for matrix-vector multiplications. However, architectural constraints hinder ACIM’s ability to support end-to-end workloads, necessitating complementary specialized digital units for essential operations like activation functions, pooling, and attention. These units are typically hardwired, lacking the adaptability needed in a rapidly evolving AI landscape. To address this challenge, we propose EAGLE, an ACIM-based architecture that integrates general-purpose Programmable Multi-Core Accelerators (PMCAs) as the sole digital accelerator, offering unprecedented flexibility and enabling seamless end-to-end execution across diverse workloads while maintaining competitive throughput. The architecture integrates state-of-the-art RISC-V Snitch clusters as PMCAs, extended with specialized instructions to enhance performance and energy efficiency. We introduce novel lightweight LUT-based ISA extensions to approximate commonly used transcendental functions and perform HW/SW co-optimization of key AI kernels, leveraging hardware extensions to eliminate control and memory instruction overhead. The flexibility of EAGLE is demonstrated across a range of model architectures, including encoder- and decoder-based transformers, a convolutional neural network, and a recurrent model (LSTM). Evaluated in 28 nm FD-SOI, EAGLE sustains 61.1/97.96 Inf/s on end-to-end MobileBERT/BERT-Large inference, achieving up to $3\times $ energy efficiency improvements over state-of-the-art 8 nm GPUs.

title: MeanCut: Greedy graph clustering by fast maximum spanning tree and degree descent criterion_supp1-3692651.pdf
authors: Zhipeng Gui
abstract: As the most typical graph clustering method, spectral clustering is popular and attractive due to its remarkable performance, easy implementation, and strong adaptability. Classical spectral clustering measures the edge weights using pairwise Euclidean similarity and resolves the optimal graph partitioning by relaxing the constraints of indicator matrix and decomposing the Laplacian matrix. However, Euclidean similarity might cause skew graph cuts when handling non-spherical clusters, and the relaxation strategy introduces information loss. Meanwhile, spectral clustering requires specifying the number of clusters, which is difficult to determine without enough prior knowledge. In this work, we propose a greedy-optimized scheme for resolving the indicator matrix using path-based similarity and degree descent criterion. It yields an indicator matrix with strictly binary entries without destructive relaxation and discretization steps. Path-based similarity can enhance the intra-cluster associations of arbitrary-shaped clusters, while degree descending is theoretically proven to be the best order to minimize our proposed objective function MeanCut. Moreover, we define a density gradient factor to separate clusters with fuzzy boundaries, and develop a fast maximum spanning tree algorithm to improve the scalability of similarity calculation. The effectiveness of MeanCut has been demonstrated on synthetic datasets and real-world benchmarks. By fusing multi-view image features, MeanCut outperforms cutting-edge subspace clustering methods in face recognition. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZPGuiGroupWhu/MeanCut</uri>.

title: Nonparametric Control Koopman Operators
authors: Petar Bevanda, Bas Driessen, Lucian Cristian Iacob, Stefan Sosnowski, Roland Tóth, Sandra Hirche
abstract: This paper presents a novel Koopman composition operator representation framework for control systems in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHSs) that is free of explicit dictionary or input parametrizations. By establishing fundamental equivalences between different model representations, we are able to close the gap of control system operator learning and infinite-dimensional regression, enabling various empirical estimators and the connection to the well-understood learning theory in RKHSs under one unified framework. Consequently, our proposed framework allows for arbitrarily accurate finite-rank approximations in infinite-dimensional spaces and leads to finite-dimensional predictors without a priori restrictions to a finite span of functions or inputs. To enable applications to high-dimensional control systems, we improve the scalability of our proposed control Koopman operator estimates by utilizing sketching techniques. Numerical experiments demonstrate superior prediction accuracy compared to bilinear EDMD, especially in high dimensions. Finally, we show that our learned models are readily interfaced with linear-parameter-varying techniques for model predictive control.

title: A Compact Slew-Rate-Enhanced Class-AB Buffer Amplifier Using Internal Current Reuse for Display Column Drivers
authors: Dong-Hyun Kim, Seong-Ro Lee, Chan-Keun Kwon, Jong-Seok Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: High-resolution and high-frame-rate display systems impose increasingly stringent timing constraints on column-driver buffer amplifiers due to the drastic reduction of 1-horizontal (1-H) time. Although slew-rate enhancement (SRE) is essential to meet these constraints, conventional SRE techniques rely on explicit slew-sensing circuits and dynamic current sources (DCSs), introducing unavoidable static current and area overheads that severely limit scalability in multi-channel column-driver ICs. This paper proposes a compact SRE class-AB buffer amplifier that improves large-signal transition speed through transient-only internal current reuse, without external sensing circuits or DCSs. Slewing detection and enhancement are embedded directly into the signal path using only two diode-connected transistors, which remain inactive during steady-state operation, achieving zero static-current penalty and negligible area overhead while preserving the traditional amplifier’s stability. Fabricated in a 0.18-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m CMOS process, the proposed amplifier achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.1\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> slew rate improvement (from 0.8 V/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s to 1.7 V/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s) under a 3.3-V supply and a 30 pF load, while maintaining an identical static current of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.2~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>A. The additional area required is only <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (0.3% increase), with no change in overall layout dimensions. Measurements demonstrate that the proposed amplifier meets the 1-H timing constraint of a 4K display operating at 120Hz. To evaluate scalability in highly replicated analog systems, this work analyzes the trade-off between slew-rate performance and implementation overhead. The results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves sufficient slew-rate improvement to meet the target timing requirement with zero additional static current and minimal area overhead, ensuring highly scalable operation for next-generation, high-resolution display column-driver ICs.

title: In-Situ Electric Field-Assisted Preparation of SiC/Epoxy Composite Coatings with Oriented Filler Structure_supp1-3691698.mp4
authors: Cheng Pan
abstract: The SiC/epoxy nonlinear conductive coating has shown great potential in DC GIS/GIL for its ability to suppress surface charge accumulation and homogenize the electric field. However, a high doping amount severely restricts its further application. In this paper, an in-situ electric field-assisted technology is introduced to fabricate epoxy-based composite coatings with aligned SiC fillers. In-situ microscopic observation and micro computed tomography (Micro-CT) characterizations reveal the 3D orientation of the coatings. A comparison with uniformly doped samples at a higher concentration shows that the oriented coatings at a lower concentration have more continuous conductive paths, facilitating carrier migration. As a result, compared with uniformly doped coatings, the oriented coatings exhibit significantly lower trap levels and higher shallow trap densities. Moreover, combining experimental and simulation results reveals that increasing the in-situ electric field amplitude can significantly enhance the permittivity and DC nonlinear conductivity in the orientation direction. This increase alleviates local electric field distortion and improves the DC flashover voltage of the samples. This technology is more effective than simply increasing the doping amount, showing great potential in the insulation design of high-voltage DC devices.

title: Parametric Modeling and GPR Imaging of Planetary Lava Tubes Constrained by Terrestrial Analogs_supp1-3692190.pdf
authors: Ling Zhang
abstract: Lava tubes are priority subsurface targets on basaltic planetary bodies, and Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) is a primary detection method. However, interpretation is often limited by forward models relying on idealized cavities, which underestimate scattering effects caused by geometric asymmetry and boundary roughness. We introduce a parametric modeling and imaging workflow constrained by terrestrial analogs. Using measured cross-sections from the Pyroduct database, we embedded realistic cavity geometries into a lunar stratigraphic model defined by correlated random permittivity. Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) simulations were performed with a 60 MHz source, followed by Kirchhoff pre-stack time migration. Unlike ideal cavities, analog-constrained models produce asymmetric diffractions and local phase discontinuities. Quantitative analysis shows that realistic morphological complexity severely degrades the signal-to-clutter ratio (SCR) of ceiling reflections, which drops from 80.67 dB in the idealized model to 25.19 dB in the analog-constrained model. Migration analysis suggests that while ceiling location and lateral extent are reliably constrained, the tube floor remains difficult to resolve. Field GPR data from a terrestrial lava tube show similar ceiling-dominant responses, validating this geometric prior model for evaluating performance in future planetary missions.

title: Edge Feature Enhancement and Multi-scale Convolutional Kernel Selection Aggregation for Fine-grained Ship Detection
authors: Yaxiong Chen, Shuo Gao, Qi Wu, Zhong Sai, GuiRong Feng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: In recent years, with the continuous advancement of high-resolution remote sensing imaging technology, the capability of perceiving maritime ship targets under complex sea conditions has been significantly improved. This holds substantial theoretical value and practical significance for enhancing maritime traffic situational awareness, strengthening the supervision of fishery activities, and safeguarding the marine environment as well as maritime security. However, due to the single imaging perspective of remote sensing images, fine-grained ship detection still faces challenges. such as large intra-class scale variations caused by varying imaging heights and ambiguous inter-class boundaries for visually similar ship types.To address this issue, we propose the Edge-Aware Feature Enhancement Module (EFEM) to strengthen the fusion of edge details and spatial contextual information of ships in remote sensing images, and design the Multi-Scale Kernel Aggregation Module (MSKAM) to improve the model’s ability to perceive ships of varying sizes. These modules effectively solve the problem of extracting discriminative features of ships. Finally, experiments conducted on the third hierarchy of the ShipRSImageNet dataset and the FAIR1M dataset demonstrate that, compared with the benchmark model, the proposed fine-grained ship detection model achieves improvements of 1.87 % and 1.72 % in the mAP50 metric, respectively. Moreover, comparative validations on the two datasets further confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in enhancing the accuracy of fine-grained ship detection.

title: Game of Coding: Coding Theory in the Presence of Rational Adversaries, Motivated by Decentralized Machine Learning
authors: Hanzaleh Akbari Nodehi, Viveck R. Cadambe, Mohammad Ali Maddah-Ali
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: Coding theory plays a crucial role in enabling reliable communication, storage, and computation. Classical approaches assume a worst-case adversarial model and ensure error correction and data recovery only when the number of honest nodes exceeds the number of adversarial ones by some margin. However, in some emerging decentralized applications, particularly in decentralized machine learning (DeML), participating nodes are rewarded for accepted contributions. This incentive structure naturally gives rise to rational adversaries who act strategically rather than behaving in purely malicious ways. In this paper, we first motivate the need for coding in the presence of rational adversaries, particularly in the context of outsourced computation in decentralized systems. We contrast this need with existing approaches and highlight their limitations. We then introduce the game of coding, a novel game-theoretic framework that extends coding theory to trust-minimized settings where honest nodes are not in the majority. Focusing on linear codes, we highlight two key features of this framework: (1) the ability to achieve a non-zero probability of data recovery even when adversarial nodes are in the majority, and (2) Sybil resistance, i.e., the equilibrium remains unchanged even as the number of adversarial nodes increases. Finally, we explore scenarios in which the adversary's strategy is unknown and outline several open problems for future research.

title: Enhancing Blockchain Proof of Stake With Active Weighted Signatures: The ADAPT Framework
authors: Jae Hyun Choi, Hobin Jang, Ik Rae Jeong, Changmin Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Proof of Stake (PoS) blockchain systems require weighted threshold signatures where participants' voting powers reflect their stakes. As stakes change dynamically through deposits and withdrawals, efficient weight and threshold adjustments are essential for maintaining system security and availability without downtime. However, existing approaches face critical limitations: (1) virtualization-based schemes require <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(w)$</tex-math></inline-formula> operations (signatures) per participant with weight <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$w$</tex-math></inline-formula>; (2) dynamic threshold / paricipants schemes do not support weighted participants; (3) schemes with both properties require trusted dealers or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(n^{2})$</tex-math></inline-formula> re-setup, causing temporary unavailability. This paper introduces Active Weighted Signature (AWS), enabling dynamic adjustments without trusted dealers or re-setup. We propose Generalized Lagrange Interpolation (GLI), encoding weights as polynomial derivatives rather than virtualized participants, and instantiate AWS through <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> by applying GLI to the Schnorr-based threshold signature <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\sf FROST}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Our implementation shows that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> achieves comparable efficiency to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\sf FROST}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for key generation, while weight and threshold adjustments complete in 4.1-22.3% of re-setup time. For uneven weight distributions, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> achieves sub-linear scaling: 49× weight difference requires only 3.29× computation versus 49× in virtualization.

title: Advanced safety filter based on SOS Control Barrier and Lyapunov Functions
authors: Michael Schneeberger, Silvia Mastellone, Florian Dörfler
abstract: This paper presents a novel safety filter framework that ensures both safety and the preservation of the legacy control action within a nominal region. This modular design allows the safety filter to be integrated into the control hierarchy without compromising the performance of the existing legacy controller during nominal operation. For a control-affine system, this is accomplished by formulating multiple Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) and Control Lyapunov-like Functions (CLFs) conditions, alongside a forward invariance condition for the legacy controller, as sum-of-squares constraints. Additionally, the state-dependent inequality constraints of the resulting Quadratic Program (QP) - encoding the CBF and CLF conditions – are designed to remain inactive within the nominal region, ensuring preservation of the legacy control action and performance. To avoid the chattering effect and guarantee the uniqueness and Lipschitz continuity of solutions, the state-dependent inequality constraints of the QP are selected to be regular. Finally, we demonstrate the method in a detailed case study involving the control of a three-phase ac/dc power converter.

title: Adversarial Domain Alignment Improves Root Biomass Prediction from SkySat Imagery
authors: Haireti Alifu, Vasit Sagan, Abuduwanli Maiwulanjiang, Supria Sarkar, Omar Al Akkad, Bishal Roy, Heng Ye, Dhandapani Raju, Henry Nguyen
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Root biomass plays a critical role in plant water uptake, nutrient acquisition, and terrestrial carbon cycling. However, estimating root biomass from satellite imagery remains challenging because roots are belowground and cannot be directly observed. Furthermore, inter-annual climate variability alters canopy spectral responses, causing domain shifts that degrade machine learning model generalization across growing seasons. In this study, we propose a domain-adaptive framework that aligns multi-year remote sensing features using a Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN), followed by regression modeling on the aligned latent features. Using 1,355 soybean plots and 160 spectral, textural, and tasseled-cap features derived from multi-temporal SkySat imagery, baseline pooled performance yielded R² = 0.02. After DANN-based alignment, R² improved to 0.79–0.81 (ΔR² ≈ +0.77), with RMSE reduced from ~0.20 to ~0.08–0.09 kg plot⁻¹ (60% reduction in RMSE). Diagnostic experiments confirm that strict cross-year generalization remains limited without target-year information. These results demonstrate that domain alignment is essential for robust multi-year root biomass estimation using remote sensing.

title: Machine Learning and Deep Learning-Based Condition Assessment of Outdoor Ceramic Insulators: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Stefano F. Stefenon, Ayman El-Hag, Mehdi Bagheri, Khaled Shaban, Behzad Kordi, Refat Ghunem, Stefan Kornhuber
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Outdoor ceramic insulators are critical components of power transmission and distribution overhead lines, providing essential mechanical support and electrical insulation for high-voltage conductors. Their reliable operation is increasingly challenged by material aging, physical defects, and surface contamination, which can lead to flashover events and subsequent power outages. In recent years, machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques have emerged as powerful, scalable, and data-driven tools for automated condition assessment of outdoor ceramic insulators, offering faster, more objective, and more consistent alternatives to traditional manual inspection practices. This paper presents a comprehensive and structured review of state-of-the-art ML/DL-based approaches for the diagnosis and condition assessment of outdoor ceramic insulators. The review contributes by organizing the field along three complementary dimensions: (i) foundational ML/DL methodologies and their adaptation to non intrusive insulator monitoring; (ii) comparative analysis of image and radiation based sensing modalities, including infrared, ultraviolet, acoustic, and radio frequency techniques, together with multimodal data fusion strategies; and (iii) a critical examination of dataset limitations, validation practices, and common misconceptions such as overfitting and insufficient field evaluation that explain contradictory results across studies. Emerging trends, including unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based inspection, synthetic data generation, and autonomous intelligent monitoring, are synthesized to identify open challenges and best practices toward reliable, interpretable, and scalable artificial intelligence (AI)-driven inspection systems.

title: A Study of the Removability of Speaker-Adversarial Perturbations
authors: Liping Chen, Chenyang Guo, Kong Aik Lee, Zhen-Hua Ling, Wu Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Recent advancements in adversarial attacks have demonstrated their effectiveness in misleading speaker recognition models, making wrong predictions about speaker identities. On the other hand, defense techniques against speaker-adversarial attacks focus on reducing the effects of speaker-adversarial perturbations on speaker attribute extraction. These techniques do not seek to fully remove the perturbations and restore the original speech. To this end, this paper studies the removability of speaker-adversarial perturbations. Specifically, the investigation is conducted assuming various degrees of awareness of the perturbation generator across three scenarios: ignorant, semi-informed, and well-informed. Besides, we consider both the optimization-based and feedforward perturbation generation methods. Experiments conducted on the LibriSpeech dataset demonstrated that: 1) in the ignorant scenario, speaker-adversarial perturbations cannot be eliminated, although their impact on speaker attribute extraction is reduced, 2) in the semi-informed scenario, the speaker-adversarial perturbations cannot be fully removed, while those generated by the feedforward model can be considerably reduced, and 3) in the well-informed scenario, speaker-adversarial perturbations are nearly eliminated, allowing for the restoration of the original speech.

title: Efficient NoC for Embedded Heterogeneous Multi-Core DSPs
authors: Wei Chen, Dake Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: At present, most networks on chips (NoCs) are designed for symmetric multiprocessing architectures for general-purpose computing. While currently designed NoCs provide considerable flexibility, they are accompanied by substantial routing delays and a relatively high cost associated with reorder buffer memory. The significant difference in data transmission length is a characteristic of heterogeneous multi-core systems. We propose an innovative NoC architecture for embedded heterogeneous multi-core digital signal processor (DSP) systems, which includes the design of top-level architecture, router, network interface (NI), and NoC pipeline. Our designed NoC architecture has the advantages of flexibility and low latency. It can not only significantly improve the transmission performance of long data packets but also improve the transmission efficiency of short data packets. Moreover, we propose efficient routing methods, which include an efficient routing algorithm, a hybrid transmission method, a packet-connected circuit transmission method, a short packet transmission method, and multicast and broadcast transmission methods. We also propose fault-tolerant data transmission mechanisms, which include a fault-tolerant data transmission algorithm and a deadlock avoidance method. Our proposed method and mechanism can improve the performance and reliability of NoC, mitigate the impact of router failures, eliminate the need for reorder buffer memory, reduce power consumption, and improve the robustness of NoC. The experiments show the performance advantages of our design in terms of latency, throughput, and silicon overhead in comparison with state-of-the-art NoCs. Our design reduced the latency, area, and power consumption by 47%, 92.6%, and 78.9%, respectively, and improved the throughput by 27%. This verifies its effectiveness in high-end applications.

title: An Event-Driven Dual-Mode 1280×1024 Cryogenic Infrared Vision Sensor for Adaptive Energy-Efficient Motion Perception
authors: Mingzhong He, Yixuan Du, Dezhen Yang, Yi Zhuo, Yufei Ai, Xiaoming Wang, Qingjiang Xia, Yacong Zhang, Zhongjian Chen, Songlin Yu, Wengao Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents an event-driven, dual-mode cryogenic infrared vision sensor that features a two-step time-encoding (TE) analog-to-digital converter (ADC) and a high-speed frame difference (FD) for motion detection. To enable always-on scene-adaptive motion perception with power optimization, the vision sensor is reconfigurable, supporting two operation modes: 1) Precise Imaging (PIM) and 2) Motion Detection (MD). In PIM mode, a two-step TE ADC is proposed for small-pixel-pitch infrared focal plane arrays (IRFPAs). The coarse conversion utilizes pulse frequency modulation (PFM) with an area-efficient true single-phase clock (TSPC) counter. This PFM employs a non-periodic-reset architecture to enable multi-subframe fusion and fine conversion of time-to-first spike (TFS), thereby improving the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) within a limited pixel area. In MD mode, a high-speed FD-based 1.5-bit event detection is implemented, using subframe coarse conversion without incurring additional memory cost or frame-rate loss. Compared with PIM mode, MD mode reduces power consumption by approximately 40% by deactivating fine conversion and partial output channels. A <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1280\times 1024$</tex-math> </inline-formula> prototype with a 15-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m pixel pitch was fabricated using a stacking process, in which the digital readout integrated circuit (DROIC) was implemented in a 0.18-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m CMOS process and interconnected with the HgCdTe IRFPA via indium bumps. In PIM mode, the prototype achieves 83.2dB SNR with a power consumption of 115.4 mW at 30.5 frames per second (fps) (1.49 pJ<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot \mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/step). In MD mode, with an 8-bit coarse conversion for event detection, the sensor consumes 58.83 mW at 976.6 fps (3.5 fJ/pixel-event), achieving the best event FoM among reported event-driven infrared vision sensors.

title: SWD-Net: A Benchmark Dataset for Small Water Body Detection from Very High-Resolution Imagery
authors: Di Liu, Chengbin Deng, Pontus Olofsson, Songkun Yan, Jiao Wang, Isabelle Hu, Yang Hong
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Small and seasonal water bodies store a substantial portion of inland surface water and play a key role in local hydrology, ecosystem resilience, and agricultural sustainability. Yet their small size, irregular geometry, and frequent seasonal variability hinder accurate detection in global water products derived from coarse-resolution imagery. To address this limitation, we present Small Water Body Detection (SWD-Net), a high-resolution, expert-annotated benchmark dataset for small water-body detection from aerial and satellite imagery. SWD-Net consists of 10,085 image–mask pairs (1,024 × 1,024 pixels) sourced primarily from NAIP, Google Earth, and LoveDA, spanning diverse climatic and land-cover conditions. Baseline experiments using five representative segmentation architectures, including U-Net, Mamba U-Net, ViT U-Net, SegFormer U-Net, and Swin U-Net, demonstrate clear architectural contrasts, with transformer-based networks achieving the most accurate delineation of fine-scale and fragmented ponds. Among them, Swin U-Net achieved the best overall performance, with an IoU of 93.53% and an F1-score of 96.56% on the test set. All data, pretrained weights, and evaluation codes are publicly released to facilitate transparent and reproducible research in fine-scale hydrological mapping, and seasonal water monitoring.

title: Risk-Sensitive Control With Multi-Armed Bandit Adaptive Sampling
authors: Brian W. Rybicki, Jill K. Nelson
abstract: We propose a risk-sensitive (RS) control algorithm that optimizes the entropic risk measure (ERM). RS control minimizes the impact of high cost but low probability outcomes in dynamic decision making. ERM is a time-consistent, convex measure of risk with tunable sensitivity. Our ERM-sensitive method combines online adaptive multistage sampling, RS upper confidence bound action selection, and sample-based estimation of ERM. Unlike many other RS control approaches, our method is applicable to problems with both discrete and continuous state spaces, and it does not require knowledge of the underlying transition probabilities. This makes our approach particularly useful in a wide variety of RS reinforcement learning domains. We show <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(\log M / M)$</tex-math></inline-formula> errorbounded convergence under mild conditions and demonstrate the numerical performance on two RS benchmark problems with discrete and continuous state spaces, respectively.

title: Spatio-Temporal Coded Multiplexing Using Spherical-Gold Codes for Fading- and Delay-Resilient Beam Isolation in Multibeam Phased Arrays
authors: Yuan Ma, Mike Ballou, Kyle Richard, Hessam Mahdavifar, Najme Ebrahimi
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: Future integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems require simultaneous multibeam operation with low-latency hardware and robust isolation under synchronization error and fading. Conventional code-division multiplexing using Walsh–Hadamard codes is extremely time-sensitive. This letter demonstrates that conventional temporal-only coded multibeam arrays result in inter-beam sidelobe level (SLL) collapsing to within a few decibels of the main lobe and varying by more than 10–20 dB over delay. By embedding moderate-length Gold sequences into a spherical spatial codebook, the proposed Spherical-Gold scheme leverages both <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/\sqrt{N}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> temporal and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/\sqrt{M}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> spatial bounds, achieving effectively <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>(<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sqrt{N} \cdot \sqrt{M}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) correlation without increasing RF complexity. Measurement results and verification have been performed using an Analog Devices ADAR3002 Ka-band 256-element receiver, where four simultaneous beams demonstrate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\ge}15$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB beam isolation with <<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm 2.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB variation under timing error and fading, while temporal-only code-division multiple-access (CDMA) degrades to −5 to −7 dB inter-beam SLL with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gt \pm 8$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB variation under timing error.

title: Switching Impulse Discharge Characteristics of Long Air Gaps with Different Electrodes and Correction in High-Altitude Environment
authors: Zhang Zhijin, Zeng Dong, Jiang Xingliang, Zhang Zhiqin, Hu Jianlin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The severe atmospheric environment at high altitudes and the variability in electrode shapes of power equipment complicate the insulation design of air gaps. In this paper, switching impulse discharge tests were carried out on 1‒5 m gaps of 15 different electrodes in 5 categories under the natural environment at an altitude of 1500 m. It is verified that the influence of electrode shape on the switching impulse 50% breakdown voltage (U50) of air gaps is independent of the gap distance (d) and absolute humidity (H). The electrode shape can cause the U50 of the 4 m gap to fluctuate in 1051.3‒1178.0 kV. Then, combined with finite element simulation, the spatial electric field non-uniformity of different electrode gaps was studied. The results show that with the increase of non-uniformity, the breakdown voltage of the gap decreases rapidly first, and then tends to be stable. Finally, the mathematical relationship among U50, d, H and electrode curvature radius (r) is obtained, which verifies that the atmospheric factors have the most significant influence on U50, followed by d and r. On this basis, gap factors (0.972–1.083) for different electrode gaps are proposed. The error between the U50 evaluation based on the gap factors and the experimental values is less than 5%.

title: Angle-Aware Real Time Transformers for Oriented Fine-Grained Ship Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images
authors: Nicolae Catalin Ristea, Anamaria Radoi
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Transformer-based detectors, such as the DETR series, have gained significant popularity for object detection due to their strong performance across a variety of benchmarks. However, these architectures are typically limited by high computational costs, restricting their deployment in real-time and resource-constrained environments. To address this limitation, Real-Time DETR (RT-DETR) was introduced, offering an effective trade-off between speed and accuracy. Despite these advances, RT-DETR models are constrained to horizontal bounding boxes and lack the ability to detect oriented objects, a critical requirement in many fields, such as remote sensing. To bridge this gap, we propose AA-RTDETRv2, an angle-aware real-time transformer, specifically designed for oriented object detection and classification. To the best of our knowledge, AA-RTDETRv2 is the first end-to-end RT-DETR-based framework capable of real-time, rotation-aware detection and classification without relying on post-processing steps. Our model introduces two key components: (1) an angle-aware multi-scale encoder that enhances orientation-sensitive feature extraction, and (2) an Angle-Aware Uncertainty-Minimal Query Selection strategy that produces high-quality, rotation-informed object queries to improve detection and classification precision. The proposed AA-RTDETRv2 architecture was evaluated on two standard remote sensing benchmarks (i.e., ShipRSImageNet and DOTA) and achieved state-of-the-art performance-speed balance in oriented object detection and identification. Our method outperforms both NMS-based detectors and prior DETR variants while maintaining inference speeds suitable for real-time applications. These results underscore the effectiveness of AA-RTDETRv2 in addressing remote sensing challenges, including dense object distributions and arbitrary object orientations. The code is accessible at https://to-be-added.

title: Enhancing Stereo Matching Domain Generalization with Adversarial Domain Alignment
authors: Shiqian Zhao, Meiqing Wu, Kangjie Chen, Yi Xie, Tianlin Li, Siew-Kei Lam, Guowen Xu, Anran Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Recently, state-of-the-art stereo-matching networks trained on large-scale synthetic data have shown remarkable performance. However, their capacity to extrapolate effectively to unseen real-world data, i.e. different domains, remains a challenge. The major difficulty resides in the unforeseeable domain gap when generalizing from synthetic data to real-world data. In this paper, we introduce ADASM, an approach using adversarial domain alignment, designed to enhance the robustness and generalization of stereo-matching networks. It mainly consists of two modules: an end-to-end robustness optimizer and a domain-invariant feature learner. First, we adapt adversarial training into the stereo-matching task to reduce models' sensitivity to the perturbation in real-world samples. By introducing worst cases into the training space, we take unseen data into account and achieve robust disparity estimation for the end-to-end model. Then, via simulating the real-world noise with gradient-based perturbation, we construct a fictitious domain, which is taken as a referential distribution of the real-world noisy data, for further domain alignment. Specifically, we propose to utilize Maximum Mean Discrepancy to realize domain regularization between the original domain and the fictitious one. Finally, we fuse all aforementioned objectives and propose a unified, simple but effective loss function that can be adapted to all stereo-matching networks. The extensive experiments show that our method achieves a superior disparity estimation performance on various real-world benchmarks, including KITTI, Middlebury, and DrivingStereo. More importantly, ADASM obtains competitive or even better performance than the fine-tuning strategy, revealing its fine-tuning-free character.

title: Fourier Neural Operator Approach for Ionospheric Phase Compensation in L-band SAR Interferometry
authors: Kazuki Yanagiya, Yutaro Shigemitsu, Takeshi Motohka, Takeo Tadono
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: The ionosphere introduces path delay phases into Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) images, degrading the accuracy of ground deformation measurements particularly for long-wavelength L-band systems. Analytical split-spectrum estimation can become unstable in non-ideal cases due to decorrelated sub-band interferograms, phase-unwrapping errors, and missing data. This letter proposes a learning-based estimator based on a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) to infer the ionospheric path delay phase from range sub-band interferograms in a single shot. Trained solely on simulated interferograms with known ionospheric phase, the model transfers to Advanced Land Observing Satellite‑2 (ALOS‑2) Stripmap-1 interferograms without retraining. With a center-frequency separation of 9.33 MHz between the sub-bands, the proposed method achieves a mean RMSE of 0.0146 on 10,000 test interferograms (normalized to [−1, 1]), evaluated against the true ionospheric phase over all pixels including no-data regions. Real-data experiments confirm transferability across interferograms with different coherence levels and geographic conditions. Further work will extend the method to a wider range of sub-band division settings, improve the physical realism of simulated ionospheric phases, and validate the approach on a broader set of real interferograms.

title: E-Link: A 256-ch Mini-Pedestal Connector for High-Density Soft Neural Interfaces_supp2-3692102.mp4
authors: Hui Fang
abstract: High-density thin-film neural interfaces face persistent packaging challenges at the electrode–electronics interface, where conventional rigid connectors impose high insertion forces, strict alignment requirements, and limited reusability. We present E-Link (Elastomeric Link), a modular 256-channel mini-pedestal connector designed to facilitate simplified, user-friendly assembly through an insertion-free, alignment-tolerant interface utilizing an elastomeric conductive interposer and a compliant threaded compression mechanism. Finite element analysis and experimental validation confirm uniform contact stress across the 16 × 16 interconnect array. The system achieves stable electrical performance, exhibiting contact impedances of 0.3-0.4 kΩ, an RMS noise floor of 2.68 ± 0.46 μV, and a connection yield exceeding 97% over 100 mating cycles. The detachable architecture reduces chronic head-mounted mass from 6.6 g to 2.8 g while maintaining safe thermal operation (30.5 °C steady-state) under a full 20 kHz sampling bandwidth. Design analysis further indicates scalability toward 1024-channel integration within the same 25-mm-diameter footprint by leveraging fine-pitch elastomeric conductive pillars. This work provides a robust and scalable connection solution for high-density flexible neural interfaces with broad application for bioelectronics.

title: Impact of the thermally forced accelerated aging process on lightning properties of ester liquids compared to conventional mineral oil
authors: Filip Stuchala, Pawel Rozga, Ian Hosier
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Transformers are devices built and designed for many years of failure-free operation. Since most of them have paper-oil insulation, it's important to check the condition of the insulation. This paper focuses on dielectric liquids. The purpose of this research is to examine the effects of thermally forced accelerated ageing on the lightning properties of natural (NE) – Paryol Electra 7426 and synthetic (SE) – Naturelle Transformer Fluid S4 I ester liquids in comparison to traditional inhibited mineral oil (IMO) – Nytro 10 XN. The procedure involves heating a liquid to a high temperature of 150°C for 168 hours in accordance with IEEE and CIGRE recommendations, cooling the liquid, and performing the experiments at lightning impulse voltage. These tests included determination of various of parameters characterizing lightning performance of liquids such as lightning impulse breakdown voltage (LIBV) supported by time to breakdown (t<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">b</sub>), inception voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub>) and acceleration voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</sub>). A point-to-sphere electrode system with a 25 mm inter-electrode gap and a negative polarity of lightning impulse voltage is being used for the tests. The measurements are performed using fresh liquids and then after one and two aging cycles. Liquids are also tested for additional aging-related factors such as AC breakdown voltage, dielectric loss factor and acidity. Measurements make allowance for correlation of changes in the lightning properties of liquids under study with the degree of ageing. According to observed data, for all the liquids under tests LIBV and Va decrease with aging. However, more variations are noted in case of mineral oil. This is most noticeable for the acceleration voltage, which for fresh mineral oil (IMO) is 310 kV, and after the second aging cycle (IMO14) 235 kV. For the tested esters, a decrease in this parameter is also noted, but it is not as drastic and amounts to approximately 15 kV for NE and 20 for SE.

title: E-Link: A 256-ch Mini-Pedestal Connector for High-Density Soft Neural Interfaces_supp1-3692102.mp4
authors: Hui Fang
abstract: High-density thin-film neural interfaces face persistent packaging challenges at the electrode–electronics interface, where conventional rigid connectors impose high insertion forces, strict alignment requirements, and limited reusability. We present E-Link (Elastomeric Link), a modular 256-channel mini-pedestal connector designed to facilitate simplified, user-friendly assembly through an insertion-free, alignment-tolerant interface utilizing an elastomeric conductive interposer and a compliant threaded compression mechanism. Finite element analysis and experimental validation confirm uniform contact stress across the 16 × 16 interconnect array. The system achieves stable electrical performance, exhibiting contact impedances of 0.3-0.4 kΩ, an RMS noise floor of 2.68 ± 0.46 μV, and a connection yield exceeding 97% over 100 mating cycles. The detachable architecture reduces chronic head-mounted mass from 6.6 g to 2.8 g while maintaining safe thermal operation (30.5 °C steady-state) under a full 20 kHz sampling bandwidth. Design analysis further indicates scalability toward 1024-channel integration within the same 25-mm-diameter footprint by leveraging fine-pitch elastomeric conductive pillars. This work provides a robust and scalable connection solution for high-density flexible neural interfaces with broad application for bioelectronics.

title: Folding Convolutional Neural Network for Rotating Machinery Fault Diagnosis
authors: Tiantian Lu, Hongbin Zhu, Jisheng Dai, Huadong Lai, Weichao Xu
abstract: One-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN)-based methods are widely used in fault diagnosis for rotating machinery. However, traditional 1D-CNN-based approaches are limited by the fixed receptive field of their convolutional kernels, which restricts their ability to capture long-range dependencies in signal data. To address the issue, a folding convolutional neural network (FCNN) is proposed. By folding the input signals before convolution, the FCNN enables a relative shift between distant information and the CNN's receptive field, allowing the model to effectively utilize both local and long-range features. Building on the FCNN, a multiview fault diagnosis framework is constructed through the integration of a transformer encoder and a long short-term memory (LSTM) network. Transfer learning is employed to optimize the initial parameters of the model. The proposed method first uses a three-layer FCNN to extract features from each channel individually, followed by feature fusion. The fused features are then analyzed by a transformer encoder, which captures global contextual relationships through self-attention, and further processed by an LSTM to model temporal dynamics. Experiments conducted on one private and three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness, robustness, noise tolerance, and advanced performance of the proposed model.

title: In-Situ Electric Field-Assisted Preparation of SiC/Epoxy Composite Coatings with Oriented Filler Structure_supp2-3691698.pdf
authors: Cheng Pan
abstract: The SiC/epoxy nonlinear conductive coating has shown great potential in DC GIS/GIL for its ability to suppress surface charge accumulation and homogenize the electric field. However, a high doping amount severely restricts its further application. In this paper, an in-situ electric field-assisted technology is introduced to fabricate epoxy-based composite coatings with aligned SiC fillers. In-situ microscopic observation and micro computed tomography (Micro-CT) characterizations reveal the 3D orientation of the coatings. A comparison with uniformly doped samples at a higher concentration shows that the oriented coatings at a lower concentration have more continuous conductive paths, facilitating carrier migration. As a result, compared with uniformly doped coatings, the oriented coatings exhibit significantly lower trap levels and higher shallow trap densities. Moreover, combining experimental and simulation results reveals that increasing the in-situ electric field amplitude can significantly enhance the permittivity and DC nonlinear conductivity in the orientation direction. This increase alleviates local electric field distortion and improves the DC flashover voltage of the samples. This technology is more effective than simply increasing the doping amount, showing great potential in the insulation design of high-voltage DC devices.

title: Distributed Task Allocation for Multi-Agent Systems: A Submodular Optimization Approach
authors: Jing Liu, Fangfei Li, Xin Jin, Yang Tang
abstract: This paper addresses dynamic task allocation in resource-constrained multi-agent systems (MASs) with sequentially updated assignments. We develop a submodular maximization framework integrated with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$q$</tex-math></inline-formula>-independence systems, demonstrating greater flexibility than conventional matroid-based constraints for modeling heterogeneous resource limitations. The proposed distributed greedy bundles algorithm (DGBA) addresses communication limitations in MASs while providing rigorous approximation guarantees for submodular maximization under a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$q$</tex-math></inline-formula>-independence system constraint, ensuring low computational complexity. DGBA achieves feasible task allocation in polynomial time with reduced space complexity compared to existing methods. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations in a micro-satellite observation scenario demonstrate that DGBA consistently outperforms benchmark algorithms in total utility, resource efficiency, and assignment stability, while maintaining real-time computational feasibility.

title: A Harmonic-Based Simultaneous Wireless Power and Data Transfer Method Using Improved DPPM
authors: Bo Liang, Zhaoyong Mao, Peizhou Liu, Bo Cheng, Shengzhao Pang, Zhengchao Yan, Yayu Ma, Jiale Wang, Yiming Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This brief proposes a Simultaneous Wireless Power and Data Transfer (SWPDT) method utilizing the third-harmonic component of a phase-shift modulated inverter for downlink communication. By dynamically adjusting the phase-shift angle, data is modulated via improved Differential Pulse Position Modulation (DPPM). A secondary-side harmonic isolation network is designed to suppres harmonic currents in the load. Theoretical analysis validates the load-independent nature of the transmission. Experimental results demonstrate a 310.45 W power transfer with 91.05% peak efficiency and a 36.3 kbps data rate.

title: Geometric-Mean De-Embedding: A Heuristic Distributed Method for Precise Characterization of RF HEMTs
authors: Mohammad Rezaei, Amirhossein Esteghamat, Elison Matioli
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: We propose a heuristic combination of conventional lumped de-embedding models [i.e., open–short (OS) and short–open (SO)] that is equivalent to a distributed model. The technique was named geometric-mean de-embedding (GM-DE), as it converges to the geometric mean of the two other methods for the single-port case. This is unlike existing distributed methods, which are often overly complicated, require additional dedicated calibration steps, and therefore have not been adopted in common practice. While lumped de-embedding has remained the mainstream method for more than three decades, our approach provides a practical yet robust pathway toward distributed de-embedding. This is of utmost importance for accurate parameter extraction of RF HEMTs, particularly for the correct estimation of key performance figures like <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$f_{\max }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.

title: Influence of Post-Arc Charge Collection on the Dynamic Voltage Distribution of Double-Break Vacuum Circuit Breakers
authors: Xian Cheng, Yuanxin Yao, Guowei Ge, Shuai Du, Hui Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Non-uniform dynamic voltage distribution (DVD) across series-connected breaks is a critical factor limiting the development of multi-break vacuum circuit breakers (VCBs) towards higher voltage levels. However, the mechanism underlying the non-uniform DVD across the series-connected breaks remains unclear. An equivalent circuit model of double-break VI was proposed in this paper based on the post-arc plasma transport characteristics derived from Particle-in-Cell (PIC) method, considering the effects of charge collection of the shields and middle electrode. Based on the established model, the charge collection characteristics under varying initial plasma densities were investigated, yielding a quantitative relationship between the charge collection on the shields and middle electrode and the resulting DVD. The study reveals that non-uniform charge collection by the shields and the middle electrode alters the charge state of external grading capacitors, significantly affecting the DVD. Quantitative analysis indicates that at an initial plasma density of 1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sup> m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup>, a 1000 pF grading capacitor reduces the voltage sharing non-uniformity to 9.4%. The accuracy of the model was validated by the strong consistency between simulation results and experimental measurements. This work provides a theoretical reference for research on the mechanism of series DVD in high-voltage vacuum switches.

title:
A Compact 3~13-GHz Injection-Locked-and-PI Based Quadrature Clock Generator with 28.9-fs Jitter and 1120 μm
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    Core Area
authors: Ruixi Yu, Qiyuan Liu, Li Wang, Zunsong Yang, Yunbo Huang, Xuan Guo, Xiaoyu Shan, Kai Cheng, Hongyu Ren, Bo Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: This work presents a compact quadrature clock generator (QCG) for wireline transceivers, with a core area of 1120 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The design achieves a quadrature error of less than 0.7° across a frequency range of 3 to 13 GHz, delivering integrated jitter of 28.9 fs at 13 GHz. It employs a two-stage coarse-fine architecture: the coarse stage consists of an injectionlocked ring oscillator with interdigitated cross-coupled pairs to isolate the output from the injection nodes. This configuration enables a relatively accurate initial quadrature relation, low power consumption, and a correct duty cycle over a wide frequency range. The fine stage incorporates an integration-mode phase interpolator, effectively correcting the quadrature relationship through I/Q phase blending. An inverter-based closedloop biasing circuit regulates the output DC level within the saturation region, ensuring linear phase blending. The complete QCG consumes less than 7.0 mW across the frequency range.

title: Optimizing the weight stable set attack with budget constraint
authors: Ruiqing Sun, Weidong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Identifying the most influential communicators as an issue of maximizing influence has become one of the most notable topics in social network analysis, as it has achieved success in viral marketing. Only by understanding our opponent's way of thinking can we effectively defend against or attack them. We particularly focus on one type of such attacks called the weight stable set attack problem with budget constraint. Given a social network, each node has a weight and removal cost. This problem is to remove a subset of the nodes whose total removal cost does not exceed a given threshold, such that the maximum weight stable set in the resulting graph is minimized. We first propose a 2<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>-approximation algorithm for this problem on the graph without odd cycles, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the approximation ratio for the algorithm of the minimum knapsack problem. Interestingly, this algorithm can be extended to general graphs. We also design a genetic algorithm for general graphs. Finally, we conducted many experiments on both artificial networks and real-world networks. For the graph without odd cycles, the results show that our performance ratio is less than 1.21. For general graphs, we compare the algorithm that extends the idea of graphs without odd cycles with the genetic algorithm. The results show that this algorithm is better than the genetic algorithm in some settings.

title: Indefinite Time-inconsistent Stochastic Linear-quadratic Optimal Control Problem with Application
authors: Min Li, Na Li, Yuan Wang, Zhen Wu
abstract: In this paper, we study a class of indefinite time-inconsistent stochastic linear-quadratic optimal control problems, where the cost functional involving two additional time-inconsistent terms. The main idea of our paper is to first establish the results of more general time-inconsistent problems under the positive definite assumption and then extend them to the indefinite case. By introducing a relaxed compensator, we derive a necessary and sufficient condition for equilibrium controls of time-inconsistent linear-quadratic problems under the indefinite assumption. The solvability results of Riccati equations are provided in some special cases. In addition, the feedback form of equilibrium control is derived. Finally, a resource investment problem in enterprise production is solvable.

publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: The hardware convergence of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) is hindered by conflicting computational paradigms: dense tensor parallelism versus asynchronous sparse dynamics. Existing unifications typically rely on inefficient spatial partitioning or mode-reconfigurable datapaths, limiting the flexibility needed by heterogeneous ANN-SNN hybrid models requiring frequent cross-domain interaction. To resolve this, we present NeuroUNI, a unified event-driven multi-core architecture. Unlike partitioned designs, NeuroUNI unifies computation at the primitive level using a novel Five-Tuple Event Model, abstracting both continuous activations and discrete spikes to naturally leverage dynamic sparsity. The architecture features a co-optimized hierarchical Macro-Micro-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>OP ISA, a superscalar SIMD-based microarchitecture, and a decentralized multi-core synchronization protocol. Validated in TSMC 28nm technology via post-synthesis simulation and on a Xilinx VCU129 FPGA prototype, NeuroUNI demonstrates competitive cross-paradigm efficiency. It achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$35.0\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.21\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> the ANN energy efficiency of the NVIDIA V100 and EyerissV2, respectively, while delivering <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.7\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> the SNN throughput of TrueNorth. In a unified mapless navigation workload, NeuroUNI attains 422.6 GOPS/W (ANN) and 190.8GSOPS/W (SNN), outperforming Loihi 1 with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$56.7\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> the throughput and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.85\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> the energy efficiency, proving the viability of a primitive/ISA-level unified silicon substrate.

title: RD-PCN: A Secure Role-Differentiated Payment Channel Network for Heterogeneous Nodes
authors: Qinghao Wang, Can Chen, Ning Lu, Wenbo Shi, Zhiquan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Scalability is a critical challenge for blockchain-based cryptocurrencies and has become a significant bottleneck in large-scale applications such as retail. Payment channel networks (PCNs) are among the most promising solutions to this challenge. However, nodes in real-world PCNs exhibit heterogeneous behaviors and capabilities, causing existing schemes to perform poorly when deployed in practical environments. In this paper, we first demonstrate, through a measurement study, the existence of node heterogeneity in real-world PCNs; and then propose RD-PCN, a role-differentiated PCN that accommodates such heterogeneity by explicitly separating node roles and responsibilities. We address two key challenges in realizing RD-PCN. To improve channel fund utilization, RD-PCN introduces multi-party payment channels (MPCs) to selectively connect nodes at the network edge. We accordingly design a PCN-compatible MPC, termed PC-MPC, representing the first practical solution for deploying MPCs in PCNs. To securely outsource routing tasks to resource-rich nodes, we design a privacy-preserving and fair routing scheme based on trusted execution environments and the proposed topology synchronization mechanism. We prove the security of PC-MPC and the routing scheme under the universally composable framework. Experiments show that RD-PCN improves the payment success ratio by at least 18.9% compared to representative schemes, while increasing fund utilization by 60%.

title: PAF-Net: Prototype-Guided Attention Fusion Network for HSI and LiDAR Data Joint Classification
authors: Fanbin Zhang, Xiaoguang Yuan, Wei Feng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data are highly complementary at the semantic level, and their fusion contributes to more accurate image classification. However, existing fusion mechanisms are often limited by unconstrained feature interactions and imbalanced learning rates across heterogeneous modalities. In this paper, a prototype-guided attention fusion network (PAF-Net) is proposed, which employs category prototypes as alignment targets to enhance feature learning and attention fusion. Specifically, a convolutionally aligned cross-modal transformer (CACMT) models local–global spatial relationships to facilitate cross-modal interaction. A confidence-weighted modality masking (CWMM) module then adaptively adjusts modality channels to ensure comprehensive feature learning for all modalities. Finally, a semantic-query-guided cross attention fusion (SQ-CAF) module exchanges semantic cues between modalities to fully mine their complementary information. Extensive experiments on three mainstream HSI and LiDAR datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed PAF-Net.
